items.q: What is the gender of 'el día' (day)?
items.why: Students rely on '-a = feminine'. Common exceptions: el día, el mapa, el problema, el sistema, el clima (many Greek-origin -ma words). These appear constantly so must be memorised.

items.q: How is 'll' typically pronounced in Spanish?
items.why: Students pronounce 'll' as English double L. In most Spanish dialects, ll is a YOD sound (like English y) or a 'zh' sound (Argentina). Never pronounced as a doubled English L.

items.q: How do you say 'I like coffee' (in general) in Spanish?
items.why: English drops the article for general likes; Spanish keeps it. For general preferences, use the DEFINITE article: me gusta EL chocolate, me gustan LOS libros.

items.q: How do you say 'I am 14 years old' in Spanish?
items.why: Correct answer: Tengo catorce años. “Soy catorce años” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
The answer examiners want is: muy grande. “un poco grande” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
Exact answer: 17. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
